1: wayyiqqāhălû kol-ʿăḏat bənê-yiśrāʾēl šilōh — 'the whole assembly of Israel gathered at Shiloh.' The transfer of the cult center from Gilgal (where the Jordan-crossing monuments stood) to Shiloh marks a new phase: the tent of meeting (ʾōhel môʿēd H168) is erected in permanent territory. nikkəbāšāh hāʾāreṣ (H3533) — 'the land was subdued/conquered'; kābaš carries the sense of trampling down, bringing under foot — not just military defeat but enforced submission (cf. Genesis 1:28 where Adam is commanded to kibšūhā, subdue the earth). The use here closes the conquest narrative: Canaan is under Israel's foot at last.
2: wayyiwwāṯərû bənê-yiśrāʾēl šebaʿ šəḇāṭîm ʾăšer lōʾ-ḥāleqû ʾet-naḥălāṯām — 'seven tribes of Israel remained who had not divided their inheritance.' yāṯar (H3498) means to remain over, be left as a surplus — a residual. The verb ḥālaQ (H2505, divide/apportion) is the dominant verb of the allotment chapters. The nāḥălāh (H5159, inheritance) they have not divided is legally theirs — the delay is procrastination, not dispossession.
3: Joshua's rebuke: ʿad-mātay ʾattem mitrappîm lāḇōʾ lārešet ʾet-hāʾāreṣ — 'until when are you slack/negligent about going in to possess the land?' rāpāh (H7503) means to let droop, to become slack — related to the idiom 'let your hands fall' (cf. Jeremiah 6:24). Joshua uses the same verb as Moses's critics used against him (Exodus 5:8,17 — 'you are idle, idle!'). The irony: Israel was enslaved for reluctance to work; now they are free and reluctant to claim their freedom's full fruit. The land is a gift they must actively receive.
4: Joshua directs each tribe to appoint three men to survey the remaining land and write its description (kāṯaḇ H3789) in a book (sēfer H5612). The written survey is a legal document — transforming geographic description into title deed. The three-per-tribe delegation (21 men for 7 tribes) ensures proportional tribal representation in the assessment process.
5: The land is to be divided (ḥālaQ) into seven portions, with Judah retaining the south and Joseph the north. The prior allotments of the two dominant tribes serve as fixed boundaries within which the remaining seven must be assigned. The spatial constraint drives the need for careful measurement and proportional assignment.
6: wəʾānōkî ʾašlîk lākem gôrāl pōh lipnê YHWH ʾĕlōhênû — 'I myself will cast the lot for you here before YHWH our God.' gôrāl (H1486) is the lot — a stone, stick, or pebble used for decision-making. The mechanism transfers human preference entirely to divine determination: lōʾ-ḥālaQ libnê yiśrāʾēl would mean human parceling; the lot means YHWH parcels. The phrase lipnê YHWH (before YHWH) at the tent of meeting is the critical locator: this is a covenant ceremony, not a land survey.
7: The Levites have no portion (ḥēleq H2506) among the tribes 'because the priesthood of YHWH is their inheritance (nāḥălātām).' The Levitical exclusion from land is the deepest expression of priestly theology: God himself, mediated through sacrificial service, is their possession. ḥēleq and nāḥălāh are near synonyms here, both meaning share/allotment — the point is that Levi's share is non-territorial.
8: The men commissioned to survey the land go out and walk through it (hālak H1980), writing descriptions (kāṯaḇ) by cities into seven sections (ḥālaqîm). The verb šūṭ (to rove, range across) is not used here; rather hālak, to walk — a deliberate, methodical traversal. The written record anticipates the modern cadastral survey: a formal inventory of territory enabling lawful distribution.
9: The men traverse the land and describe it in a document with seven divisions, then return to Joshua at Shiloh. The practical geography — towns, boundaries, watersheds — is recorded to enable the lot to be applied to real territory. Without accurate description, the lot's result would be unimplementable. The combination of human survey and divine lot models the integration of careful human work and divine sovereignty.
10: wayyapîl yəhôšuaʿ gôrāl lahem bəšilōh lipnê YHWH — 'Joshua cast the lot for them at Shiloh before YHWH.' The lot is cast (nāpal H5307, lit. 'to fall') — the lots 'fall' rather than are 'chosen,' emphasizing that the outcome descends rather than rises. The sanctuary location (lipnê YHWH) converts the lot into a divine verdict. Proverbs 16:33: 'The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from YHWH.'
11: Benjamin's lot 'came up' (wayyaʿal gôrāl H5927) — the lot fell between the territory of Judah and the territory of Joseph. The positional description — geographically sandwiched — accurately describes Benjamin's narrow north-south strip. The language 'came up' for the lot suggests the lot was drawn from a container (shaken in a fold of cloth and drawn out).
12: Benjamin's northern boundary is described from the Jordan westward: wayyaʿal gəḇûlām (the boundary 'goes up,' H1366 H5927) — geographic language treats territory as a three-dimensional surface, with 'going up' meaning moving toward higher elevation or toward the watershed. gəḇûl (H1366, boundary/border) is the key term of these chapters — the line that defines the inherited portion.
13: The boundary descends (yāraḏ H3381) toward Luz (Bethel) and passes toward Ataroth-addar. The alternating 'going up' and 'going down' language tracks the actual topography of the central hill country. Bethel — where Jacob saw the ladder (Genesis 28:19) — is on Benjamin's border with Ephraim, and its history of divine encounter gives this boundary geographic and theological weight.
14: The western boundary turns southward from Kiriath-baal (= Kiriath-jearim, where the ark will later rest, 1 Samuel 7:2). The identification of a city by two names — its pre-Israelite name and its Israelite name — is a feature of these chapters, preserving geographic memory across the conquest transition.
15: The southern boundary of Benjamin runs from the edge of Kiriath-jearim westward to the spring of the Waters of Nephtoah (likely Lifta north of Jerusalem). The precise boundary description — springs, valleys, and identifiable landmarks — reflects survey accuracy preserved across centuries of transmission.
16: The boundary goes down to the foot of the hill facing the Valley of Ben Hinnom, north of the Valley of Rephaim. The Valley of Ben Hinnom (gêʾ ben-hinnōm) will become Gehinnom in later Jewish usage — the dump where refuse burned — and eventually lend its name to the NT concept of hell. Here it is simply a topographic marker on Benjamin's southern border.
17: The boundary curves from En-shemesh (Spring of the Sun) to En-rogel (Fuller's Spring) — two named springs that served as permanent geographic anchors. Spring names encode function or history; ʿên (H5869/H5878, spring) is the most reliable landmark in an arid landscape, unchanging across centuries.
18: The boundary continues over the slope opposite the Arabah, then descends to the Arabah. The Arabah (hāʿărāḇāh H6160) is the Jordan Rift Valley — the deepest geographic feature of the region. This corner of Benjamin's territory touches the eastern lowlands, giving the tribe access to multiple ecological zones.
19: The boundary passes to the north shore of the Salt Sea (yām hammeleḥ H3220 H4417) at the mouth of the Jordan. The Salt Sea (Dead Sea) terminates Benjamin's southeastern corner. meleḥ (salt) describes its chemical character; the same term appears in Ezekiel 47:8-11 where the Dead Sea is healed but its salt-marshes preserved.
20: The Jordan forms the eastern boundary. The four-sided description is complete: north = ridge above Beth-aven, west = Kiriath-jearim ridge, south = Ben Hinnom / Jordan mouth, east = the Jordan. The territorial precision serves both legal and theological functions — the lot's result is recorded as binding covenant deed.
21: The cities of the tribe of Benjamin are now listed by name. The distinction between gəḇûl (boundary, the linear border) and ʿārîm (cities, the discrete settlements within the territory) marks the transition from boundary description to town inventory — two complementary ways of defining inheritance.
22: Beth-arabah, Zemaraim, Bethel — the city list begins with places whose names echo broader biblical narratives. Bethel (house of God, H1008) appears both as a boundary marker (vv13) and as a city in the list, reflecting its central importance as a cult site at the tribal border.
23: Avvim, Parah, Ophrah — cities in the central hill country. Ophrah will later be associated with Gideon (Judges 6:11), demonstrating how these town-list chapters of Joshua provide the geographic foundation for the narratives of Judges. The inheritance that seems merely administrative carries the seeds of the subsequent history.
24: Chephir-ammoni and Ophni — two more Benjaminite towns. The town list format (ʿārîm with no narrative) is legal, not literary; it functions as title record. The names' preservation across centuries of transmission — even where origins are obscure — reflects the scribal care given to covenant-land records.
25: Gibeon, Ramah, Beeroth — notable cities. Gibeon (H1391) is where the Gibeonites made their peace with Israel (Josh 9) and where YHWH gives Solomon wisdom (1 Kings 3:4-5). Ramah (H7414, height) is the birth and burial city of Samuel (1 Samuel 1:19; Joshua 25:1). Benjamin's town list is simultaneously a legal catalog and a narrative forecast.
26: Mizpeh, Chephirah, Mozah — Mizpeh (H4709, watchtower/lookout) becomes one of the major tribal gathering places in Judges and Samuel (Judges 20:1; 1 Samuel 7:5-12; Joshua 10:17). The naming of a watchtower-city points to the defensive topography that characterized Benjamin's territory on the edge of the central ridge.
27: Rekem, Irpeel, Taralah — the list continues. These cities in Benjamin's territory fill out the picture of a densely settled, strategically located tribe — sitting athwart the main routes between north and south, between the Mediterranean coast and the Jordan Valley.
28: Zelah, Haeleph, Jebus (that is Jerusalem), Gibeah, and Kiriath — fourteen cities in total. The identification of Yəḇûs (H2982) as Jerusalem (Yərûšālāyim) is significant: Jerusalem is listed as a Benjaminite city, though it remained Jebusite until David's capture (2 Samuel 5:6-9). Its inclusion here as an uncaptured city within Benjamin's allotment sets up the canonical anticipation of its eventual conquest and theological centrality.
